coredata.dc:title: A novel Nitrogen-Doped Water-Soluble carbon dot for highly selective Recognition of Mg2+ by chelation conjugation enhancement effect (CCE) and its application in Synergistically enhanced photodynamic antibacterial
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Kezhen
Tian, Xiaoyong
Xu, Peipei
Guang, Shanyi
Xu, Hongyao
coredata.dc:description: Magnesium (Mg2+) is crucial for various biological processes, making real-time Mg2+ monitoring essential for health. Traditional organic fluorescent sensors face challenges such as complex synthesis and poor water solubility, limiting their application. In contrast, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) offer a promising alternative due to their simple synthesis, stability, and biocompatibility. This study presents water-soluble N-CDs synthesized via a hydrothermal method, which show high selectivity for Mg2+ detection through a chelation-conjugation enhancement (CCE) effect. The nitrogen doping also enhances photosensitivity, enabling the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for photodynamic therapy. Notably, Mg2+-chelated N-CDs exhibit superior photodynamic antibacterial activity. Theoretical and experimental analyses reveal that the CCE significantly improves electron transfer, leading to enhanced photosensitizing properties. This work offers a novel and efficient strategy for dual-functional Mg2+ detection and photodynamic therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnesium
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Photosensitization
Reactive oxygen species
Photodynamic antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photodynamic antibacterial activity of N-CDs and their Mg2+ complexes
N-CDs for specific optical sensing performance of Mg2+
Mechanistic Investigation of specific Mg2+ detection by N-CDs
N-CDs for biological imaging of Mg2+
Photosensitized oxygen activation and ROS detection
Detection of reactive oxygen species in cells mediated by N-CDs and N-CDs-Mg2+
Exploration of the photosensitization performance of N-CDs in photodynamic therapy
Mg2+ Chelation enhanced photodynamic antibacterial performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM and HRTEM images of N-CDs.
(a) Survey XPS analysis of N-CDs; (b-d) High-resolution C 1 s spectra, high-resolution N 1 s spectra, high-resolution O 1 s spectra; (e) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs; (f) Raman spectra of N-CDs.

(a) UV–VIS and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs. The inset shows the fluorescence image of N-CDs; (b) Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (c) Changes in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of Mg2+ (0–800 μM), λem = 490 nm; (d) Fluorescence intensity ratio as a function of Mg2+ concentration, the inset shows the linear fitting curve for the concentration range of 0–250 μM; (e-f) Selectivity of N-CDs towards different metal ions (500 μM).
(a) Optimal conformations of N-CDs, N-CDs-Mg2+, and N-CDs-Ca2+; (b) HOMO-LUMO orbitals and energy gaps of optimal conformations of N-CDs, N-CDs-Mg2+, and N-CDs-Ca2+.
(a) Fluorescence imaging of (a) cells; (b) zebrafish treated with N-CDs (5 mg/mL) and different concentration gradients of Mg2+; Scale bar: 50 μm.

(a) UV–VIS absorption spectra of TMB treated with N-CDs under different conditions (N-CDs: 5 mg/mL, illumination condition: 365 nm LED irradiation for 1 min); (b) Corresponding images of TMB under different conditions; (c) Investigation of specific ROS generated by N-CDs through the use of radical scavengers (tryptophan: for 1O2; superoxide dismutase, SOD: for ·O2 −; mannitol: for ·OH; catalase: H2O2). EPR characterization of ROS generated by N-CDs (5 mg/mL, 365 nm LED irradiation for 1 min): (d) TEMP for 1O2; (e) DMPO for ·O2 − (in methanol); (f) DMPO for ·OH (in water).

Fluorescence images of ROS generated through N-CDs mediated photosensitization using the ROS fluorescent probe DCFH-DA (during fluorescence imaging, cell nuclei were stained with Hoechst 33342. Scale bar: 25 μm).

N-CDs mediated PDT process: (a) Dark and Phototoxicity of N-CDs at different concentrations (0–5 mg/mL); (b) Dark and phototoxicity of N-CDs-Mg2+ (0–5 mg/mL); (c) Live/dead cell staining of 4 T1 cells treated with N-CDs (0–5 mg/mL) and N-CDs-Mg2+ (0–5 mg/mL) using acridine orange/propidium iodide (AO/PI) kit scale bar: 100 μm.

(a) The growth conditions of E. coli and S. aureus treated with N-CDs (0–5 mg/mL) and N-CDs-Mg2+ (0–5 mg/mL; Mg2+: 500 μM) under different conditions (b) Quantitative analysis of the growth inhibition of the two bacteria under different treatments (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01). Lighting time: 1 h; LED parameters: 365 nm, 3 V/3 W.
Graphical abstract for the synthesis of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs).

head.ce:title: A novel Nitrogen-Doped Water-Soluble carbon dot for highly selective Recognition of Mg2+ by chelation conjugation enhancement effect (CCE) and its application in Synergistically enhanced photodynamic antibacterial

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, and Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, and Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, and Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnesium (Mg2+) is crucial for various biological processes, making real-time Mg2+ monitoring essential for health. Traditional organic fluorescent sensors face challenges such as complex synthesis and poor water solubility, limiting their application. In contrast, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) offer a promising alternative due to their simple synthesis, stability, and biocompatibility. This study presents water-soluble N-CDs synthesized via a hydrothermal method, which show high selectivity for Mg2+ detection through a chelation-conjugation enhancement (CCE) effect. The nitrogen doping also enhances photosensitivity, enabling the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for photodynamic therapy. Notably, Mg2+-chelated N-CDs exhibit superior photodynamic antibacterial activity. Theoretical and experimental analyses reveal that the CCE significantly improves electron transfer, leading to enhanced photosensitizing properties. This work offers a novel and efficient strategy for dual-functional Mg2+ detection and photodynamic therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnesium (Mg2+) plays multiple pivotal roles in biological systems, participating in crucial biological activities including enzyme activity regulation, cell signaling, nucleic acid stability modulation, and energy metabolism processes[1–5]. Particularly in terms of skeletal health, Mg2+ serves as a vital component of bone tissue, playing essential roles in bone formation, maintenance, and repair, crucial for maintaining bone density and strength[6–9]. Therefore, the maintenance of Mg2+ homeostasis is paramount for normal physiological functions and overall health, contributing significantly to the stability of cellular environments, normal cellular functions, and overall well-being[10,11].

Currently, fluorescence probes, including organic fluorescence probes and nanomaterial-based probes, are commonly used for Mg2+ detection[12–17]. These probes typically rely on changes in the fluorescence intensity or fluorescence signal enhancement/quenching to detect Mg2+ [18,19]. However, the complex structures and poor water solubility of most traditional organic molecule probes have limited their application in biological contexts[20–22]. Therefore, the development of a simple synthesis method for water-soluble inorganic nanomaterials to achieve high-selectivity detection of Mg2+ is crucial[23–26].

In previous studies, fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) have been widely utilized in the fields of metal ion detection, biosensing, and bioimaging owing to their simple synthesis methods, chemical structural stability, and excellent biocompatibility[27–32]. By selecting different precursors, including natural and synthetic compounds, researchers have synthesized a series of carbon dots with excellent properties[33–37]. These carbon dots not only exhibit different optical properties during the synthesis process, but their surface functional groups, size, morphology, and fluorescence properties can also be adjusted according to the characteristics of the precursors. Currently, there is a growing interest in fluorescence “turn-on” carbon dots research due to their broader practical applications compared to quenching-type carbon dots. For example, CDs prepared by Huang et al. achieve Cu2+ fluorescence enhancement detection through the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) effect[38]. Similarly, CDs prepared by Hassan et al. enable Zn2+ detection through chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF)[39]. Recently, Sadhanala et al. have developed nitrogen-doped CDs for Mg2+ detection in aqueous media and successfully applied them for intracellular Mg2+ detection, although the detection sensitivity is not particularly high[24].

Photosensitizing oxygen activation materials play a crucial role in a variety of photodynamic applications in biomedical, analytical, and energy fields[40–44]. Carbon-based photosensitizers are attractive due to their low cost and high stability. Among them, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) exhibit the highest activity compared to other carbon nanomaterials and molecular photosensitizers[45,46]. Their outstanding photosensitizing oxygen activation capability makes these water-soluble CDs promising photosensitizers for photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy and other applications[28,30,47,48].

Based on the above research background, this study synthesized a novel high-water-soluble multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots via a one-step hydrothermal method. These carbon dots exhibit high selectivity for detecting Mg2+ through chelation conjugation enhancement effect. Additionally, rational nitrogen doping confers photosensitivity to the prepared carbon dots, allowing the generation of reactive oxygen species under excitation light irradiation for photodynamic antibacterial purposes. At the same time, through chelation conjugation enhancement effect (CCE), CDs bound with Mg2+ exhibit increased conjugation structure, resulting in enhanced electron transfer capability. Under equivalent light exposure conditions, they demonstrate higher capacity for generating reactive oxygen species. Subsequent antibacterial experiments further confirm the superior antibacterial efficacy of Mg2+-chelated carbon dots. This provides a research foundation for the development and application of novel high-efficiency photosensitizers enhanced by metal ions.

2,4-Dihydroxybenzaldehyde, 1, 2, 4, 5-Benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride, Quinine sulphate, and metal salts like Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Hg(ClO4)2·4H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, CdCl2·2··5H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Al2(NO3)3·9H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, KCl, NaCl and AgNO3 were obtained from Adamas Chemicals. 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2,7-Dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), Acridine Orange (AO)/Propidium Iodide (PI), and Hoechst 33,342 were procured from Adamas-life. Mannite, tryptophan, catalase, and superoxide dismutase (SOD) were procured from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd, located in Shanghai, China. Rose Bengal (RB), 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), and 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was obtained from Macklin Chemicals (USA). Other chemicals were purchased as analytical-grade reagents from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation (China) and were used as received without further purification. All experiments were conducted using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) throughout.

The morphology of the CDs nanoparticles was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, utilizing a JEM-2010 instrument from Japan. The particle size was also characterized by a nano particle size analyzer (Nano ZS, Malvern, UK). The functional groups of CDs were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) within the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1, utilizing a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 instrument from the United States. The Raman spectra of carbon dots were tested for defect characterization using a micro-Raman spectrometer of the inVia Reflex model from Renishaw, United Kingdom. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the structure and surface composition of carbon dots, utilizing the Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi instrument from the United States. The UV–visible spectrum of the carbon dots was obtained using a Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) with a quartz cell (1 cm x 1 cm). Fluorescence spectra were measured using an LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) employing the perpendicular illumination method. The generated reactive oxygen species were measured using EPR (EMX plus-10/12, Bruker, Switzerland), in which TEMP and DMPO (100 mM) were used as trapping agents for 1O2 and·O2 − (·OH), respectively. Cellular imaging as well as detection of reactive oxygen species were performed by fluorescence microscopy.

Synthesis of N-CDs with slight modifications based on literature, 2 mmol of 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and 0.5 mmol of 1,2,4,5-benzenetetraamine tetrahydrochloride were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, stirred thoroughly until completely dissolved, and then transferred to a 30 mL hydrothermal reactor. The reaction was carried out in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the reactor was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, and the solution was poured out and centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter to obtain a deep yellow solution. Finally, the transparent yellow filtrate was dialyzed for 1 d using a 500–1000 Da dialysis bag, and the dialysate was stored in the refrigerator for future use (Scheme 1 ).

After freeze-drying the N-CDs stock solution, prepare a 5 mg/mL solution for metal ion detection. All metal ions are prepared as 1 mM stock solutions. Specifically, take 3 mL of the diluted N-CDs solution and add different amounts of the metal ion stock solution according to the required concentration for the spectral measurements. Similarly, titrate the diluted N-CDs solution with different concentrations of Mg2+, and collect fluorescence spectra for sensitivity studies.

We employed density functional theory (DFT) calculations using Gaussian 09, Revision D.01, particularly utilizing the B3LYP/6-31G/LANL2DZ theoretical level, to optimize the structural configurations of N-CDs sensors and their complexes with magnesium ions to elucidate the high selectivity mechanism of carbon dots towards Mg2+. Furthermore, we computed the binding model of Ca2+, which has the most similar structure and is most likely to cause interference, with N-CDs. Additionally, we optimized the structural configurations of different nitrogen-doping types (graphitic N, pyridinic N, and pyrrolic N) and calculated their singlet and triplet energy levels to evaluate their photosensitization effect.

Add sequentially into the culture dish citric acid-sodium citrate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 3.5), TMB (200 mg/L), and N-CDs (5 mg/mL) or N-CDs-Mg2+ (5 mg/mL; Mg2+: 500 μM), then dilute with deionized water to 2 mL. Subsequently, illuminate with a purchased UV LED lamp (365 nm, 3 V, 3 W) for 1 min, transitioning the color from colorless to blue. Measure the characteristic absorption at 652 nm before and after light exposure using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

Using EPR to determine the types of reactive oxygen species produced. TEMP and DMPO (100 mM) were respectively used as traps for 1O2 and ·O2 − (as well as ·OH). Specifically, add N-CDs (5 mg/mL) and then irradiate with a UV LED (365 nm; 3 V; 3 W) for 1 min before recording the EPR spectrum. Similarly, further determination of the types of reactive oxygen species present was conducted using ROS-specific scavengers. Specifically, tryptophan, mannitol, superoxide dismutase, and catalase were used to eliminate generated 1O2, ·OH, ·O2 −, and H2O2, respectively.

Mouse breast cancer cells (4 T1) cells were cultured in RPMI medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 100 IU/mL penicillin and streptomycin, in an atmosphere of 95 % air and 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. Before conducting the experiment, replace the culture medium with fresh RPMI. For Cell Imaging, to validate the high selectivity detection of Mg2+ in cells using N-CDs, cells were cultured with different concentration gradients of Mg2+ for a period of time. Subsequently, the culture medium and cells were washed, and after culturing with N-CDs (5 mg/mL) for 2 h, cells were placed under a fluorescence microscope for cell imaging observation. Assessment of PDT performance of N-CDs using standard CCK-8 assay. 4 T1 cells were co-incubated with different concentrations of N-CDs and RB for 12 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well, and cells were incubated for another 4 h in the incubator. After washing cells with PBS, the OD values at 450 nm of each well were measured using a microplate reader. Assessment of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production levels using the non-fluorescent probe 2,7-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA; Adamas Life). 4 T1 cells were cultured in a six-well plate for 48 h, followed by treatment with N-CDs (10 mg/mL) for 12 h. Subsequently, the cells were irradiated under a 365 nm UV lamp for 1 min. After washing the cells twice with PBS, they were co-incubated with 5 mg/mL DCFH-DA in the incubator for 20 min. The cells were then analyzed by fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodynamic antibacterial activity of N-CDs and their Mg2+ complexes
ce:section.ce:para: Selection of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus as model microorganisms for antibacterial experiment. Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were cultured in 2 % Luria Bertani (LB) broth at 37 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, bacterial suspensions were diluted to a certain concentration (106). Then, the diluted bacterial suspensions were mixed with different concentrations of N-CDs (5 mg/mL) and N-CDs-Mg2+ (5 mg/mL; Mg2+: 500 μM) in a 24-well plate, followed by exposure to UV-LED light (365 nm, 1 h). Afterwards, 100 μL of the mixture was transferred to environments containing 2 % LB broth and 1.5 % agar, respectively, and incubated at 37 °C for another 12 h. The samples were observed and counted under a microscope.

The microstructure and size of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, N-CDs exhibited uniform monodisperse quasi-spherical morphology with a narrow size distribution. Statistical analysis of N-CDs size using Nano Measurer software yielded an average size of ∼ 3.28 ± 0.11 nm (see the inset in Fig. 1b). Additionally, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 1a, the HRTEM image of N-CDs revealed distinct lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon. In addition, the XRD pattern of the prepared N-CDs shows two prominent peaks at 2θ = 22.4° and 43.6°, which correspond to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite, respectively (Fig. S1). This is consistent with the TEM results.

Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), further analysis was conducted on the specific structure and surface composition of N-CDs. As shown in the XPS spectra, peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s appeared at 285, 400, and 532 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 a), with the proportions of the three elements being 75.01 %, 13.20 %, and 11.80 %, respectively. Subsequently, the peaks were deconvoluted into distinct components. Two C 1 s peaks were observed at approximately 284.6 and 285.6 eV, corresponding to the chemical bonding energy levels of C=C/C–C and C–O/C–N, as shown in Fig. 2b[49]. The N 1 s peak exhibited three characteristic peaks at approximately 398.3 eV, 399.2 eV, and 400.5 eV, corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, and graphitic nitrogen, respectively (Fig. 2c)[50]. The O 1 s spectrum showed three peaks at 532.5 eV, 533.1 eV, and 533.5 eV, attributed to C=O, C-OH, and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2d). Through clear analysis of the XPS spectra, successful nitrogen doping of the carbon dots was convincingly demonstrated. To further characterize the structural composition of the N-CDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to analyze the functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2e, characteristic absorption peaks at 1046, 1255, 1604, 1751, 2933, 2850, 3035, 3356, and 3433 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of −C-OH, C-N, C=C, C=O, –CH3, –CH2, N-H, and –OH, respectively. The FT-IR results also confirmed the findings of the XPS analysis, both indicating the presence of oxygen and nitrogen surface groups in the NCDs, as well as nitrogen doping. The degree of graphitization was determined through Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2f. The two peaks at 1358 and 1592 cm−1 correspond to the characteristic peaks of the D and G bands, respectively. The ID/IG ratio is approximately 0.45, indicating a higher degree of graphitization and fewer defects in the prepared N-CDs. The Raman analysis results are consistent with the graphite lattice observed in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy of N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs for specific optical sensing performance of Mg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optical sensing performance of N-CDs, we first studied their UV–VIS absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the shoulder peak of N-CDs at 215 nm corresponds to the π-π* electronic transition in the conjugated structure. The two distinct absorption peaks at 275 nm and 350 nm correspond to the n-π* electronic transitions of surface oxygen and nitrogen functional groups[51], respectively N-CDs exhibit two excitation wavelengths at 320 nm and 385 nm, with an emission wavelength of 490 nm at the maximum excitation wavelength. Additionally, they exhibit cyan-green emission under UV irradiation. Fig. 3b shows that N-CDs have excitation-wavelength-independent fluorescence emission behavior, which may be due to the relatively uniform size and surface defect structure of the N-CDs. Using quinine sulfate as a reference compound, the relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs is calculated to be 24.2 %. Before investigating the detection of Mg2+, the stability of N-CDs (Nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots) under different pH values, salt concentrations, and over time was studied. It was observed that as the pH increased from 2.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs also increased, then gradually decreased with further increases in pH value. This phenomenon could be attributed to deprotonation effects (Fig. S2a). Additionally, the salt stability of N-CDs was further investigated by exposing them to high ionic strength NaCl environments. The fluorescence intensity remained essentially unchanged under different concentrations of NaCl (Fig. S2b), indicating good salt stability. Finally, the time stability was examined by measuring the fluorescence intensity after placing the N-CDs for 1 to 7 days. The fluorescence intensity remained largely unchanged, indicating good time stability (Fig. S2c).

By adding specific concentrations of different metal ions (500 μM) to a diluted solution of N-CDs at optimal concentration, the changes in fluorescence intensity under 365 nm excitation were observed to evaluate their selectivity. It was found that only the addition of Mg2+ caused a significant enhancement in the fluorescence emission peak at 490 nm (as shown in Fig. 3e and f). Furthermore, the inclusion of common amino acids failed to produce any fluorescent response (Fig. S3a), indicating that N-CDs exhibit good selectivity for Mg2+ compared to other metal ions and amino acids. Additionally, Ca2+, which is the most common interfering ion with a similar structure to Mg2+, had almost no effect (inset of Fig. 3f). This is because the carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of N-CDs have a strong affinity for Mg2+. Additionally, this chelation is further enhanced by the incorporation of nitrogen atoms. The incorporation of nitrogen increases the electron density of the entire system through delocalization, thereby improving the selectivity for Mg2+. Next, we evaluated the anti-interference performance of N-CDs for Mg2+ detection, as shown in Fig. S3b. From the figure, it can be seen that N-CDs still respond well to Mg2+ in the presence of other metal ions, indicating their well anti-interference capability. Finally, we investigated the sensitivity of N-CDs for detecting Mg2+. By adding different concentrations of Mg2+ to the N-CDs diluted solution, we monitored the changes in fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 3c. Data fitting was performed based on Fig. 3c to determine the limit of detection (LOD) and linear concentration range for Mg2+ using N-CDs. The linear concentration range is 0–250 μM, as determined by the fitting data from Fig. 3c. The LOD was calculated to be 7.6 μM using the 3σ/K theoretical method from IUPAC, where K is the slope of the fitted curve and σ is the standard deviation of multiple blank samples (Fig. 3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic Investigation of specific Mg2+ detection by N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the specific sensing mechanism of N-CDs for Mg2+, we analyzed the spectral changes of N-CDs after adding different concentrations of Mg2+ using UV–VIS spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S4c, with the increase in Mg2+ concentration, the peak at 350 nm exhibited a significant shift. The absorption peak at 350 nm corresponds to the oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CDs, indicating that the n-π* electronic transition channel (C=O, O-H, N-H) of N-CDs interacts with Mg2+. This interaction results in chelation, which enhances the conjugated structure and causes a red shift in the absorption wavelength. This observation is consistent with the chelation and aggregation mechanism of N-CDs in the presence of Mg2+ ions reported in the literature. Induced by the chelation of N-CDs with Mg2+ may change the morphology and hydrodynamic size of the entire system, we conducted comparative analyses of the states of N-CDs before and after chelation with Mg2+ using a nanoparticle size analyzer and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Figs. S4a and S4b. From the images, it is clearly seen that the pure N-CDs are uniformly dispersed as nanodots with sizes of approximately 5 nm. However, the TEM images of N-CDs after chelation with Mg2+ show significant aggregation, with the hydrodynamic size increasing to 50 nm. This further supports the chelation-enhanced conjugation (CCE) process. Finally, according to the experimental results, the chelation model provided (Fig. S4d) indicates that the oxygen-containing functional groups of the N-CDs chelate with Mg2+, which increases the degree of conjugation of the system and improves its stability. To further verify the coordination mechanism between N-CDs and Mg2+, the changes in their corresponding infrared absorption peaks before and after coordination were analyzed, as shown in Fig. S5a. It is clearly observed that when N-CDs bind with Mg2+, the absorption peak at 1751 cm−1 corresponding to C=O disappears, indicating the formation of a coordination bond with Mg2+. Additionally, the characteristic absorption peaks of –NH2/–OH groups (3325 cm−1) and the benzene ring skeleton (1625 cm−1) shift to higher wavenumbers. This is because the coordination of Mg2+ reduces the electron density of the –NH2/–OH groups, making them more electronegative, which enhances hydrogen bonding and increases the vibration frequency of the amino and hydroxyl groups. Furthermore, the electronic effects of the amino and hydroxyl groups may alter the electron density of the benzene ring, especially through conjugation to the C=C double bond in the ring, causing the C=C vibration peak to shift to a higher wavenumber. In addition, the change in the zeta potential of N-CDs after chelation with Mg2+ was studied (Fig. S5b). It can be observed that after chelation with Mg2+, the zeta potential shifted from −12.46 mV to −0.67 mV, which is likely due to the binding of Mg2+ with the surface functional groups of N-CDs, resulting in the consumption of negative charges, this further validates the above results.

To further validate the specific detection of Mg2+ by N-CDs through the CCE effect, we optimized the corresponding structures using density functional theory (DFT) and calculated their HOMO-LUMO energy gaps. Additionally, we selected Ca2+ as a common interfering ion for comparison. The computational results are shown in Fig. 4 a and b. In Fig. 4a, the optimal structural configurations of N-CDs, N-CDs-Mg2+, and N-CDs-Ca2+ are displayed from left to right, respectively. Fig. 4b shows the electron distribution of the HOMO-LUMO orbitals and the energy gaps (ΔE) relative to their optimal structures. From Fig. 4b, it can be seen that the electron cloud density overlap in N-CDs is relatively small, with a HOMO-LUMO energy gap of ΔE = 0.4289 eV. After chelation with Mg2+, the electron cloud density is evenly distributed throughout the system, increasing electron delocalization, which stabilizes the system and reduces the energy gap to 0.2461 eV. Conversely, when Ca2+ is used as the model, the redistribution of the electron cloud density is limited, and the energy gap of the system remains nearly unchanged. Therefore, the interference of Ca2+ in the detection of Mg2+ is relatively small. This could be because Mg2+, with their smaller radius and higher charge density, tend to form stronger coordination bonds and tighter chelation structures. Mg2+ can form stable multidentate coordination structures with multiple coordination atoms (such as nitrogen and oxygen). In contrast, Ca2+, due to their larger radius and lower charge density, have relatively weaker coordination ability, and the coordination bonds formed are not as stable as those of Mg2+. The above results verify the specificity of the N-CDs for detecting Mg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs for biological imaging of Mg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The high selectivity of N-CDs for Mg Mg2+ sensing was validated above. Next, we investigated whether N-CDs could be used for fluorescence imaging in vitro and in vivo. Cultured 4 T1 cells and zebrafish were pretreated with different concentration gradients of Mg2+ (100 μM, 200 μM, and 500 μM) for 1 h, washed, and then further incubated with N-CDs (5 mg/mL) for 2 h before being observed under a fluorescence microscope. The results are shown in Fig. 5 a and b (a for cellular fluorescence imaging; b for live zebrafish fluorescence imaging). As seen in the figures, N-CDs alone exhibited weak fluorescence, while the fluorescence signal intensity of the cells and zebrafish treated with different concentration gradients increased progressively. Thus, N-CDs can accurately locate within cells and monitor Mg2+ concentration changes in real time. This demonstrates the potential of N-CDs as a powerful tool for studying the role of Mg2+ in biological physiological processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosensitized oxygen activation and ROS detection
ce:section.ce:para: According to relevant literature, it has been confirmed that N-CDs, as a novel carbon-based photosensitizer, exhibit excellent photosensitizing properties. These properties are primarily associated with the types of nitrogen doping (graphitic nitrogen, pyridinic nitrogen, and pyrrolic nitrogen). In the principle of photosensitization, the degree of triplet state activation of the photosensitizer is closely related to its photosensitizing performance. Therefore, we chose ΔEST as the main factor to measure the triplet state activation of N-CDs (generally, a smaller ΔEST facilitates the ISC process and subsequent triplet state activation, enhancing photosensitizing performance). We initially selected two simplified models of N-CDs proposed and widely accepted by Rogach et al. for theoretical computational analysis. These models include the coronene and pyrene structures with one nitrogen atom doped as graphitic nitrogen, pyridinic nitrogen, or pyrrolic nitrogen. The results indicate that, regardless of the N-CDs nitrogen doping calculation model based on coronene or pyrene, graphitic nitrogen exhibits the lowest ΔEST (Figs. S6 and S7), suggesting its significant impact on the triplet state activation of N-CDs. Additionally, we calculated the HOMO and LUMO orbital energy levels and the electrostatic potential of the N-CDs nitrogen-doped models based on coronene or pyrene, as shown in Figs. S8 and S9. It can be observed that in both models, graphitic nitrogen exhibits the lowest HOMO-LUMO energy gap and the highest electrostatic potential. A smaller energy gap results in closer energy levels of the singlet state and triplet state, increasing the probability of excited state electrons transitioning from the singlet state (S1) to the triplet state (T1). This makes the ISC transition process more likely to occur. Similarly, a higher electrostatic potential can enhance spin–orbit coupling, facilitating the ISC process. This further indicates that graphitic nitrogen favors ISC transitions, thus endowing N-CDs with excellent photosensitized oxygen activation performance. Based on the above analysis, the proposed photosensitization mechanism is shown in Fig. S10.

The theoretical calculations preliminarily verified that N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) have photosensitive oxygen activation capabilities. Next, we will further confirm through experiments that N-CDs can effectively produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon photosensitization, as shown in Fig. 6 af. Firstly, we selected TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) as a colorimetric probe, which can be oxidized by ROS generated from photosensitization, resulting in a blue product with a strong absorption peak at about 652 nm. As shown in Fig. 6a and b, N-CDs (5 mg/mL) were added to a citrate-citric acid buffer containing TMB, and then irradiated with a low-power UV LED lamp (365 nm, 3 V, 3 W) for 1 min. It was observed that the TMB system with added N-CDs and UV irradiation exhibited a significant absorption peak at 652 nm and the solution turned a noticeable blue color. In contrast, the TMB solution systems without added N-CDs and without UV irradiation did not show the characteristic absorption peak of oxidized TMB, and the solution remained colorless. This indicates that N-CDs can only produce reactive oxygen species under UV light irradiation, resulting in the oxidation of TMB. Subsequently, the specific reactive oxygen species (ROS) responsible for the coloration of TMB were studied using specific ROS scavengers. Tryptophan was used to scavenge 1O2, mannitol was used for ⋅OH, SOD was used for ⋅O2 −, and catalase was used for H2​O2. As shown in Fig. 6c, 1O2, and ⋅O2 − are considered the main ROS generated by the photosensitization of N-CDs. Finally, the photosensitization performance of N-CDs was further characterized using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR). For the EPR study, 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was used to capture 1O2, and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used to capture signals of ⋅O2 −​ and ⋅OH. As shown in Fig. 6d–f, the characteristic 1:1:1 triplet signal observed from TEMP indicates the production of 1O2. Additionally, the 1:1:1:1 quartet signal captured by DMPO in methanol and the less pronounced1:2:2:1 quartet signal in water also indicate that N-CDs produce ⋅O2 − and minimal amounts of ⋅OH upon photosensitization. Therefore, the EPR results are consistent with the ROS scavenging study results shown in Fig. 6c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of reactive oxygen species in cells mediated by N-CDs and N-CDs-Mg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Given the good photosensitizing performance of N-CDs, they may play a role in photodynamic therapy and photodynamic antibacterial applications. Before studying photodynamic therapy, we first investigated the photosensitizing performance of N-CDs within cells. Hoechst 33,342 was used as a nuclear stain, and a DCFH-DA assay kit was used to detect reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells. In the presence of intracellular esterase, DCFH-DA is hydrolyzed to DCFH, which can be oxidized by ROS to form highly fluorescent DCF. Therefore, DCFH-DA can be used to further confirm the production of ROS within cells. As shown in Fig. 7 , there is significant production of ROS in cells following PDT with N-CDs (5 mg/mL), whereas cells treated with N-CDs alone or light alone show almost no fluorescence. These results indicate that the N-CDs-mediated photosensitization process in PDT can effectively produce ROS, making it suitable for photodynamic therapy. Additionally, we found that N-CDs (5 mg/mL) chelated with Mg2+ (500 μM) produce more ROS, which is consistent with our earlier explanation that chelation with Mg2+ enhances the conjugated structure of N-CDs. This enhancement increases the overall electron transfer capability, which facilitates the production of more ROS, thereby improving the efficacy of PDT.

To further verify that the photosensitizing performance of N-CDs is improved after chelating with Mg2+, we used TMB as a chromogenic agent to compare the oxidation effect of N-CDs and N-CDs- Mg2+ on TMB under the same concentration and light exposure time (absorption peak at 652 nm). As shown in Fig. S11, it can be seen that N-CDs-Mg2+ also exhibit a significant absorption peak at 652 nm. Moreover, under the same light exposure time, the absorption peak at 652 nm for N-CDs- Mg2+ is stronger than that for N-CDs alone, and the solution appears a deeper blue color. These findings further confirm that N-CDs have enhanced photosensitized oxygen activation capabilities after chelating with Mg2+, enabling the production of more reactive oxygen species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the photosensitization performance of N-CDs in photodynamic therapy
ce:section.ce:para: We first explored N-CDs-mediated photodynamic therapy (PDT) in tumor cells. Specifically, 4 T1 mouse breast cancer cells were used as the target, and cell viability was measured using the CCK-8 assay to evaluate the PDT performance. After incubating 4 T1 cells with different concentrations of N-CDs (0–5 mg/mL) and different concentrations of Mg2+-added N-CDs (0–5 mg/mL) for 12 h, the cell viability for N-CDs was higher than 90 %, and for N-CDs-Mg2+, it was higher than 95 % (Fig. 8 a and b). The N-CDs-Mg2+ showed better biocompatibility and lower dark toxicity, indicating that the addition of Mg2+ improved the biocompatibility of N-CDs. This could be due to the biological activity of Mg2+ in promoting cell proliferation. Additionally, when the cells were irradiated continuously with an LED lamp for 5 min (365 nm, 3 mW/cm2), the cell survival rate for N-CDs-Mg2+ decreased significantly, outperforming the pure N-CDs. This further demonstrates that Mg2+ formed a stable chelate with N-CDs, and the chelation enhanced the conjugation degree and electron density distribution, thereby improving the electron transfer capability to produce more reactive oxygen species (ROS) for the PDT process. To further confirm this structure, live/dead cell staining was performed using acridine orange-propidium iodide (AO/PI) kit. As shown in Fig. 8c, the results confirmed that the PDT performance of N-CDs- Mg2+ was superior to that of pure N-CDs, as evidenced by the significant increase in the number of dead cells under the same irradiation conditions. The above results are consistent with the previously mentioned ROS detection experiments in cells and theoretical calculation results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mg2+ Chelation enhanced photodynamic antibacterial performance
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have demonstrated that N-CDs chelated with Mg2+ exhibit enhanced photosensitizing performance, capable of generating more reactive oxygen species (ROS) under the same conditions, resulting in superior PDT efficacy. Next, we verified the practical application of the enhanced photosensitizing performance of Mg2+-chelated N-CDs through antibacterial experiments. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the number of viable bacteria in the plates treated with the photosensitizer and LED irradiation was significantly lower than that in the plates without the photosensitizer or with only light irradiation. Additionally, it can be seen that N-CDs chelated with Mg2+ exhibited better antibacterial effects compared to N-CDs alone. Through quantitative analysis, the bacterial growth inhibition efficiency of E. coli and S. aureus treated with N-CDs was 72 % and 75 %, respectively. However, for the N-CDs- Mg2+ experimental group, the growth inhibition efficiency was 95 % and 98 %, respectively (Fig. 9b). Therefore, we conclude that Mg2+-chelated N-CDs possess enhanced and highly efficient antibacterial performance, which is likely due to the production of more ROS under the same conditions, leading to excellent antibacterial effects. This also aligns with the results of the above cell experiments.

In conclusion, this study successfully synthesized novel water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method. These N-CDs demonstrate high selectivity for Mg2+ detection through a chelation conjugation enhancement effect. Additionally, nitrogen doping imparts significant photosensitivity to the N-CDs, enabling the generation of reactive oxygen species under light irradiation. This property is further enhanced upon chelation with Mg2+, resulting in superior photodynamic antibacterial activity. Theoretical calculations and experimental analyses confirm that the chelation-induced conjugation enhancement effect improves electron transfer capabilities, highlighting the potential of these N-CDs as efficient photosensitizers. This work provides a new strategy for developing high-performance, multifunctional photosensitizers for biomedical applications.

Kezhen Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xiaoyong Tian: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Peipei Xu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shanyi Guang: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Hongyao Xu: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21771036 and 52372282)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112842

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
